title: Illustrate AI Decoded: Essential Tips & Tricks for Success
description:

transcript: Welcome back to the Illustrate AI Tutorial series.  In today's video, I'll be sharing useful tips and tricks with you.  Whether you're a designer, a crafter, or just curious about the potential of AI, this guide is for you.  I have a plus unlimited subscription, which means I can generate and upscale images without using any credits.  Illustrate AI, provided by Design Bundles, is an artificial intelligence system created to produce images from natural language text.  Users can simply make a request in everyday language, and the AI will interpret it, generating an image that corresponds to the request.  A prompt is the text that you give to the AI as a starting point or instruction.  It's like telling a paint to which you want them to paint, or giving a writer the first sentence of a story and asking them to continue it.  The AI takes this text prompt and then creates something new based on that instruction, such as generating an image.  Let's say we want a picture of a bunny in a meadow. Someone new might just write.  A bunny. This isn't clear enough. What does the bunny look like? Is it white, brown, or spotted? Is it jumping, sitting, or eating?  Are there flowers or other animals near the bunny? How does the grass in a meadow look? Is it a sunny day or is it cloudy?  Illustrate AI can't guess what you're thinking. You have to say what you want clearly.  By providing more details, you ensure that the picture comes out the way you want it.  Illustrate AI will improvise less, resulting in fewer errors in the image.  To make a good prompt, start with the main subject you want in the picture, then add some details about it.  Tell about what's behind it, the background, how the light looks, and how the whole scene feels, the mood.  You can write up to 1,000 characters, but shorter prompts often work better.  Try to say what you want in less than 20 words.  If you don't have a clear idea of what you want, use more general words like a dog, or a flower.  If you have a clear picture in your mind, be very specific in your words, such as a golden retriever dog, or a rose.  You can search for types of dogs, or types of flowers on Google, or you can ask chat GPT to provide you with a list of those subjects.  So a formula for a prompt could be the subjects, subject details, the background, the color, lighting, art style, the mood.  The subject refers to what you desire to see in the image.  A common mistake people often make is not providing enough detail when describing the subjects.  Subject details include details about what the subject looks like, what it does, and any specifics related to the subject.  The background.  Consider the background.  Is it a plain backdrop, a landscape, a home interior, a wall, or are there other subjects present?  Colors.  Consider the colors of the subject.  Is it orange, black, or does the entire image have a specific color theme like an orange blue theme or a black and white theme?  Lighting.  Lighting is a crucial factor in creating a successful image, as any photographer would tell you.  Lighting keywords can significantly impact how the image looks.  Is it morning light, golden hour light, neon light, or studio light, art style?  Identify the art style.  Is it a photo, a digital painting, an oil painting, a watercolor painting, or a 3D render?  The mood.  Decide on the desired mood for the image.  Should it be mysterious, dreamy, magical, dramatic, spooky, romantic, cozy, or rustic?  I usually start with a simple prompt specifying what I want, its appearance, and the desired style.  Generate four images to observe the outcomes.  Not all prompts succeed every time.  The goal is to obtain a statistical insight into their capabilities.  Add one or two words at a time, and then create another four images to see how those additions affect the results.  Let's test our idea using a straightforward prompt.  A cat.  As you can see, it generates all different types of cats and styles.  Let's narrow it down by adding subject details and color.  Prompt.  A white Burmese cat.  Let's narrow it down even further by specifying a background.  Prompt.  A white Burmese cat with a forest in the background.  Let's specify the lighting type by adding.  Golden hour.  Now, let's add an art style like surrealism.  Finally, let's set the mood of the image, maybe a prehistoric mood.  To enhance our illustration, we can modify the prompt by incorporating flowers,  which could add more interest and charm to our image.  By incorporating more descriptive keywords in the prompt, we can narrow down the sample of cats.  Initially, we asked for any image of a cat.  Then, we specified a white Burmese cat.  Afterward, we included details about the background, lighting, art style, and mood.  The Matic Association.  When using Illustrate AI, be aware of its thematic associations.  For instance, mentioning the word Halloween might lead to images with associated elements,  such as extra pumpkins or bats in the scene.  At times, this can be useful, but if you don't want certain associations,  you can specify words in the negative prompt, like pumpkins or bats.  You'll notice that it eliminates most of them, but due to its strength,  some associations might still appear in the outputs.  To entirely avoid these, consider using alternative terms.  Instead of Halloween, describe specifics like, dark background,  the specific type of hatch you want, spooky, and so on.  I'm not suggesting that we stop using the word Halloween altogether.  I'm simply pointing out that certain words might include other items related to that word.  Halloween is indeed excellent for various illustrations, and it's concise.  However, in one particular instance, I wanted a white blank mug on a Halloween background,  but instead, the mug featured a carved pumpkin face.  Illustrate AI is a tool with its strengths and weaknesses.  Being aware of its capabilities can help avoid frustration and determine where to focus.  Over time, with improvements from better trained models,  we can expect increased understanding of prompts and generation of images with fewer errors.  Strengths, proficient in generating a diverse range of art styles,  both realistic and illustrative.  Particularly adept at creating portraits and close-ups,  excelling and capturing intricate details with minimal errors in such scenarios.  Weaknesses, struggles with textual elements, such as words and letters.  Occasional inaccuracies may arise when counting items, like,  generating six fingers instead of four or miscalculating the number of feet on animals,  five feet or three instead of four.  Difficulties can also arise when dealing with subjects far in the distance, small items,  or tiny flying creatures like birds and bats.  The performance can vary based on the style and number of attempts,  so it's essential not to expect perfect results on the first try, as these weaknesses persist.  To minimize errors in the generated image, craft the prompt using the following strategies.  Use portraits or close-ups, focus on generating images with portraits or close-ups,  as Illustrate AI performs better with these types of subjects, capturing details more accurately.  Simple subjects, opt for subjects that don't have many elements to count or intricacies to avoid counting errors.  Keep the composition straightforward and uncluttered.  Avoid textual elements, refrain from including words or letters in the prompt to prevent inaccuracies in text generation.  Negative prompt, use the negative prompt to indicate what elements you don't want to appear in the image, reducing the.  Likelihood of unwanted elements being generated.  By employing these approaches, you can enhance the accuracy and quality of the images generated by Illustrate AI  and avoid common errors associated with complex scenes, textual elements, and counting intricacies.  When you mention an artist's name in the prompt,  sometimes the AI may attempt to include a signature or watermark in the generated image.  During its training, the AI observed that many paintings have signatures on them,  and it may try to incorporate that name into the image generation as if it were a signature.  Even if I include my name in the prompt, the AI might still attempt to add it as a signature or watermark,  even though I am not a painter, and my images were not part of their training data.  Using a square ratio often yields better results than portrait or landscape.  While portrait might make characters appear taller or elongate than X,  landscape can make them look wider.  Depending on the subject, this may be fine in some cases, but can lead to obvious distortions in others.  Thus, a square ratio generally delivers more consistent and improved outcomes compared to both portrait and landscape.  Illustrate AI has difficulty with detailed color instructions.  For instance, it can manage a scenario where there's an orange cat beside a white dog since there are two colors.  However, adding a third color, like a red curtain in the background, can confuse it.  For clearer results, it's best to mention no more than two colors.  If possible, use dark or light instead of mentioning specific colors in the prompt.  For example, you can describe the subject as wearing a dark dress against a light background to avoid excessive color details in the prompt.  The importance of words in a prompt follows a specific pattern.  The words at the beginning of the prompt hold more significance, as demonstrated in our case with the orange cat.  These words are often placed at the front to emphasize their importance.  However, as the prompt lengthens, the importance of individual words diminishes.  In theory, if the prompt were, a cat and a dog, it would carry a 100% weight.  The word cat would hold around 60% power, and dog would have approximately 40% power.  Nevertheless, when we expand the prompt to, a cat with a black cat and a dog in the forest under cinematic light.  The weight is distributed among the words.  Consequently, the cat no longer retains its 60% power and may now have only 20% to 30% power.  Similarly, the dog might have around 15% power, and other words in the prompt would receive even smaller proportions of the overall weight.  And that concludes today's video tutorial.
